prompt: The total variation of f on [a,b] is the supremum, over all partitions P, of the sum of |Δf| over the subintervals of P. f has bounded variation if this number is:
explanation: Step 1: The definition takes the worst-case (largest) accumulated absolute change over any slicing. Step 2: If that worst-case total is still finite, f is BV. Final answer: Finite.

prompt: A function with a bounded derivative on [a,b] has bounded variation, with total variation at most (sup |f'|)·(b−a). Conversely, a BV function need not have a bounded derivative (it may not even be continuous). A function that oscillates infinitely often with amplitude not tending to zero fast enough has:
explanation: Step 1: Each oscillation contributes a fixed positive amount to the variation sum; infinitely many non-summable contributions make the sup infinite. Step 2: Classic example: sin(1/x) near 0 (suitably extended). Final answer: Infinite total variation.

prompt: Every function f of bounded variation can be written as the difference of two increasing functions (Jordan decomposition). One increasing function is the total variation function v(x) accumulated from a up to x; the other is v − f. This decomposition is useful because:
answer: Many properties of BV functions (differentiability a.e., jumps, etc.) reduce to the corresponding properties of monotone functions.
choices: Many properties of BV functions (differentiability a.e., jumps, etc.) reduce to the corresponding properties of monotone functions.
It shows that BV functions are always continuous.
It implies that f is differentiable everywhere.
It gives an explicit formula for the derivative.
explanation: Step 1: Monotone functions are differentiable a.e., have left and right limits everywhere, at most countably many discontinuities, etc. Step 2: The decomposition passes these properties to f. Final answer: Many properties of BV functions (differentiability a.e., jumps, etc.) reduce to the corresponding properties of monotone functions.

prompt: Conversely, the difference of any two increasing functions has bounded variation (variation at most the sum of the total variations of the two increasing functions). Therefore the class of BV functions on [a,b] is exactly:
answer: The set of all differences of increasing functions on [a,b].
choices: The set of all differences of increasing functions on [a,b].
The set of all continuous functions.
The set of all differentiable functions.
The set of all functions with bounded derivative.
explanation: Step 1: If f = g − h with g,h increasing then for any partition V(f,P) ≤ V(g,P) + V(h,P) ≤ V(g) + V(h) < ∞. Step 2: The Jordan decomposition gives the other inclusion. Final answer: The set of all differences of increasing functions on [a,b].

prompt: Every BV function on [a,b] is differentiable almost everywhere. This follows from the Jordan decomposition plus the fact that monotone functions are differentiable a.e. (a classical theorem usually proved via the rising sun lemma or Vitali covering). The derivative f' is:
answer: Lebesgue integrable, but integrating it may recover only part of f (the absolutely continuous part).
choices: Lebesgue integrable, but integrating it may recover only part of f (the absolutely continuous part).
explanation: Step 1: Monotone ⇒ differentiable a.e. ⇒ difference of two monotone is differentiable a.e. Step 2: The integral of f' recovers the absolutely continuous part; jumps and singular continuous increase are invisible to f'. Final answer: Lebesgue integrable, but integrating it may recover only part of f (the absolutely continuous part).

prompt: A BV function can have at most countably many discontinuities, and every discontinuity is a jump discontinuity (left and right limits exist). This follows because the total variation bounds the sum of the absolute sizes of all jumps: if there were uncountably many jumps the sum would be infinite, or if a discontinuity were not a jump the oscillation would force infinite variation in a neighborhood. Consequently the set of discontinuities of any BV function is:
explanation: Step 1: Each jump of size ≥ ε contributes at least ε to the total variation; only countably many such. Smaller jumps can be grouped by size 1/n; still countable union. Step 2: Non-jump discontinuities would create positive oscillation on every neighborhood, contradicting finite variation when summed over a partition. Final answer: At most countable.

prompt: Absolutely continuous functions are those BV functions that have no singular part: f(x) = f(a) + ∫_a^x g with g ∈ L^1. They satisfy the fundamental theorem of calculus everywhere the derivative exists. A continuous BV function that is not absolutely continuous (e.g. the Cantor function) has derivative zero a.e. yet increases. This shows that absolute continuity is strictly stronger than 'continuous + BV' and is exactly the condition needed for:
answer: f to be the indefinite integral of an integrable function (full FTC).
choices: f to be the indefinite integral of an integrable function (full FTC).
explanation: Step 1: The definition via ε-δ on collections of intervals is equivalent to being an indefinite integral. Step 2: The Cantor function violates the ε-δ control (it concentrates all its increase on a set of measure zero). Final answer: f to be the indefinite integral of an integrable function (full FTC).

prompt: When the integrator α has bounded variation, the Riemann–Stieltjes integral ∫ f dα can be defined (provided f and α have no common discontinuities). Integration by parts holds in the Stieltjes sense. If α is the Cantor function, the integral ∫ f dα 'sees' only the values of f on the Cantor set in a certain averaged way. This illustrates that BV integrators allow:
answer: Singular continuous 'measures' (increase on sets of measure zero) in addition to absolutely continuous and jump parts.
choices: Singular continuous 'measures' (increase on sets of measure zero) in addition to absolutely continuous and jump parts.
explanation: Step 1: The Jordan decomposition splits α into increasing parts; each increasing function induces a Lebesgue–Stieltjes measure that may be singular. Step 2: The integral against the Cantor function is integration against its associated singular continuous measure supported on the Cantor set. Final answer: Singular continuous 'measures' (increase on sets of measure zero) in addition to absolutely continuous and jump parts.

prompt: A function f on [a,b] is absolutely continuous if for every ε>0 there is δ>0 such that for any finite collection of disjoint subintervals whose total length is less than δ, the total variation of f across those intervals is less than:
choices: The length of the whole interval.
explanation: Step 1: The definition is the ε-δ control on the sum of |Δf| over any finite disjoint collection of intervals, whenever the sum of lengths <δ. Step 2: This is stronger than uniform continuity (which controls only one interval at a time). Final answer: ε.

prompt: The Cantor function is continuous and of bounded variation on [0,1] but not absolutely continuous. This is because it increases by a total of 1 while being constant on the complement of the Cantor set (a set of measure 1). One can cover the Cantor set (measure zero) with open intervals of total length <δ for any δ, yet the image under the Cantor function still covers almost the whole [0,1], so the total change is close to 1, which cannot be made <ε for small ε. This violates the absolute continuity ε-δ condition and shows that:
answer: Absolute continuity is strictly stronger than 'continuous and BV'.
choices: Absolute continuity is strictly stronger than 'continuous and BV'.
All continuous BV functions are absolutely continuous.
The Cantor function has unbounded variation.
Absolute continuity is the same as uniform continuity.
explanation: Step 1: The Cantor function maps a null set onto a positive-length interval. Step 2: The covering intervals have small total length but the oscillation (change) across their images is large, violating the definition. Final answer: Absolute continuity is strictly stronger than 'continuous and BV'.

prompt: Any Lipschitz function ( |f(x)−f(y)| ≤ K |x−y| ) on [a,b] is absolutely continuous. Indeed, the sum of |Δf| over any collection is ≤ K times the sum of lengths, so one can take δ = ε/K. This shows that Lipschitz is a convenient sufficient condition for absolute continuity. The converse is false: √x on [0,1] is absolutely continuous but not Lipschitz (its derivative blows up at 0).
choices: False; Lipschitz functions need not be absolutely continuous.
explanation: Step 1: The Lipschitz condition directly bounds the total variation on any collection by K times total length. Step 2: Choosing δ=ε/K works. The example √x shows absolute continuity does not imply a bounded derivative or Lipschitz. Final answer: True.

prompt: A function f is absolutely continuous on [a,b] if and only if f(x) = f(a) + ∫_a^x g(t) dt for some g ∈ L^1([a,b]). In that case g equals f' almost everywhere. This is the Lebesgue fundamental theorem of calculus and shows that absolutely continuous functions are exactly:
answer: The indefinite integrals of integrable functions.
choices: The indefinite integrals of integrable functions.
explanation: Step 1: If f is the integral of g, then the total change over a collection of intervals equals the integral of g over their union, which is ≤ ∫ |g| over a set of small measure and can be made small by integrability of g. Step 2: The converse (any AC f arises this way) is a theorem; the derivative supplies the density g. Final answer: The indefinite integrals of integrable functions.

prompt: If f is absolutely continuous then f is differentiable a.e. and f(x) = f(a) + ∫_a^x f'(t) dt. This is the full-strength fundamental theorem for the Lebesgue integral. For a general BV function the same formula recovers only the absolutely continuous part of f; the jumps and singular continuous increase are invisible to the integral of the derivative. This is why one must add the adjective 'absolutely continuous' when stating the strongest form of the FTC.
choices: False; the FTC holds for all BV functions.
True only if f is monotonic.
False; absolutely continuous functions need not be differentiable a.e.
explanation: Step 1: AC ⇒ BV ⇒ differentiable a.e. (Lebesgue). The representation as integral of the derivative is the content of the theorem characterizing AC functions. Step 2: For general BV the singular parts are missed by ∫ f'. Final answer: True.

prompt: Absolutely continuous functions map Lebesgue null sets to Lebesgue null sets (Lusin's condition (N)). This follows because they are indefinite integrals: the image measure is controlled by the integral of |f'| over the preimage, which has measure zero when the preimage does. The Cantor function violates (N) (maps null set onto [0,1]). This property is important in change-of-variable formulas and in the theory of Sobolev spaces.
choices: False; absolutely continuous functions can map null sets to positive measure.
explanation: Step 1: f(E) for m(E)=0 has m(f(E)) ≤ ∫_E |f'| which is zero when m(E)=0 because f' integrable. Step 2: The Cantor function provides the counter-example for the weaker BV class. Final answer: True.

prompt: Every absolutely continuous function is of bounded variation (variation ≤ ∫ |f'|). The converse is false; the Cantor function is continuous and BV but singular. In the Lebesgue decomposition of a BV function one splits off the absolutely continuous part (integral of f') from the singular part (jumps plus singular continuous). Absolute continuity means the singular part is zero.
choices: False; absolutely continuous functions need not be BV.
False; the Cantor function is absolutely continuous.
explanation: Step 1: AC ⇒ indefinite integral of integrable function ⇒ total variation finite (bounded by ∫ |g|). Step 2: The Cantor function is the standard example of continuous singular BV function. Final answer: True.

prompt: The Radon–Nikodym theorem says that if a signed measure ν is absolutely continuous with respect to a σ-finite measure μ (ν(E)=0 whenever μ(E)=0), then there is an integrable density g such that ν(E) = ∫_E g dμ. When μ is Lebesgue measure on an interval this recovers exactly the characterization of absolutely continuous functions as indefinite integrals. Thus absolute continuity of functions is the special case of absolute continuity of measures with respect to Lebesgue measure.
choices: False; Radon–Nikodym has nothing to do with absolute continuity of functions.
True only when μ is finite.
False; the density g must be continuous.
explanation: Step 1: The indefinite integral of g defines a measure absolutely continuous w.r.t. μ with Radon–Nikodym derivative g. Step 2: The converse (existence of g) is Radon–Nikodym; on the line it translates directly into the function being AC. Final answer: True.

prompt: The Lebesgue differentiation theorem asserts that for an integrable function f, the average of f over balls centered at x tends to f(x) as the radius tends to zero, for:
choices: Every x at which f is continuous.
explanation: Step 1: The theorem holds at Lebesgue points, which comprise a set of full measure. Step 2: It can fail on a null set that may be dense. Final answer: Almost every x (Lebesgue measure).

prompt: A point x is a Lebesgue point of an integrable f if the averages of |f(y) − f(x)| over balls centered at x tend to zero. At such points the differentiation theorem holds with the value f(x). For any f ∈ L^1 the set of non-Lebesgue points has measure zero. This is the content of the Lebesgue differentiation theorem in its strong form. It implies that 'the value at almost every point is the limit of its averages around the point'.
choices: False; the set of Lebesgue points may have measure zero.
False; the theorem requires f bounded.
explanation: Step 1: The proof shows that the set where limsup of averages of |f−f(x)| > ε has measure controlled by the integral of |f−f(x)| over a preliminary set, which can be made small. Step 2: Thus almost every point is a Lebesgue point. Final answer: True.

prompt: For the characteristic function χ_E of a measurable set E, the Lebesgue differentiation theorem (or Lebesgue density theorem) says that at almost every x ∈ E the density lim (m(B(x,r) ∩ E)/m(B(x,r))) equals:
explanation: Step 1: χ_E (x) =1 for x in E, so the average (which is exactly the proportion of the ball in E) tends to 1 a.e. in E. Step 2: Similarly tends to 0 a.e. outside E. Final answer: 1.

prompt: The Hardy-Littlewood maximal function Mf(x) is the supremum over r of the average of |f| over the ball of radius r at x. It is used to control the limsup of averages in the proof of differentiation. Although Mf may not be integrable, it satisfies the weak-L^1 inequality: the measure of the set where Mf > λ is at most (C/λ) times the L^1 norm of f. This weak bound is the key quantitative ingredient that makes the differentiation theorem work.
choices: False; Mf is always integrable when f is.
False; the maximal function is not needed for differentiation.
explanation: Step 1: The weak-type (1,1) bound follows from a Vitali covering argument or Calderón-Zygmund decomposition. Step 2: It replaces the 'Markov inequality' that would be used if Mf were strong L^1. Final answer: True.

prompt: If f is in L^1, Mf need not be in L^1 (e.g. f = χ_{[0,1]} / |x| log^2 |x| near 0 suitably cut off produces a maximal function with logarithmic divergence). The weak bound is sharp in the sense that there exist f in L^1 with Mf not in L^{1,weak + \varepsilon} for any \varepsilon>0. Nevertheless the weak bound suffices for differentiation because one only needs to control the measure of 'bad' level sets for the limsup, which can be made small by choosing a preliminary set on which the integral of |f − c| is small.
choices: False; Mf is always in L^1 when f is in L^1.
False; the maximal function is never in weak L^1.
explanation: Step 1: Standard counter-examples show Mf ∉ L^1 even for f in L^1 with compact support. Step 2: The proof of differentiation never requires strong integrability of Mf; the weak bound plus a preliminary approximation (Lusin or density of continuous functions) is enough. Final answer: True.

prompt: In higher dimensions the proof of Lebesgue differentiation uses the Vitali covering lemma: from a fine covering of a set by balls one can select a disjoint subcollection such that the union of the 5-times enlarged balls still covers the original set (up to a null set). Combined with the weak bound on the maximal function, this controls the measure of points where the limsup of averages exceeds a threshold. In one dimension an analogous selection (rising-sun lemma or direct greedy selection of maximal intervals) works. The dimensional constant 5 (or 3 in some versions) is harmless because it only affects the constant in the weak bound.
choices: False; Vitali covering is not used in the proof.
True only when the dimension is 1.
False; the enlargement factor must be 1.
explanation: Step 1: Vitali lets you pass from an arbitrary vitali cover to a disjoint subcollection whose controlled enlargements cover the set. Step 2: The measure of the enlarged union is bounded by a dimensional multiple of the sum of measures of the selected balls; the weak inequality then bounds that sum in terms of the integral of |f|. Final answer: True.

prompt: If ν is a measure absolutely continuous with respect to a σ-finite measure μ (ν ≪ μ), the Radon-Nikodym theorem gives a density g such that ν = g μ. The differentiation theorem for measures says that the Radon-Nikodym derivative g can be recovered as the limit of ν(B(x,r))/μ(B(x,r)) as r→0, for μ-almost every x. When μ is Lebesgue measure this is exactly the Lebesgue differentiation theorem applied to g. This unifies the 'derivative of a function' with the 'derivative of a measure'.
choices: False; the differentiation theorem applies only to Lebesgue measure.
True only when g is continuous.
False; Radon-Nikodym and differentiation are unrelated.
explanation: Step 1: The ratio of measures in small balls is the natural 'derivative' of ν w.r.t. μ. Step 2: The theorem asserts that this ratio tends to the RN density μ-a.e. Final answer: True.

prompt: Absolutely continuous functions satisfy Lusin's condition (N): they map Lebesgue null sets to Lebesgue null sets. This follows from the representation f(x) = f(a) + ∫_a^x g and the fact that m(f(E)) can be bounded using the integral of |g| over E (via the area formula or change of variables for AC functions, or directly from the definition). The Cantor function (continuous, BV, derivative 0 a.e.) maps the null Cantor set onto an interval of positive length, violating (N). Thus absolute continuity is precisely the condition that guarantees (N) for BV functions.
choices: False; Lusin's (N) is unrelated to absolute continuity.
explanation: Step 1: For AC f the change over small intervals is controlled by the integral of |g|; summing over a covering of a null set E gives m(f(E)) controlled by ∫_E |g| =0. Step 2: The Cantor function is the standard counter-example showing that continuity + BV is not enough for (N). Final answer: True.

prompt: For 1 ≤ p < ∞, a measurable function f belongs to L^p if:
explanation: Step 1: The definition is that the p-th power is integrable (finite integral). Step 2: For p=∞ the condition is essential boundedness. Final answer: ∫ |f|^p dμ < ∞.

prompt: In L^p spaces, two functions that differ on a set of measure zero are identified (they represent the same element). This is because the integral (hence the norm) does not see values on null sets. Consequently statements about elements of L^p are always understood to hold:
choices: On a set of positive measure.
explanation: Step 1: Changing f on a null set does not change any L^p norm or integral. Step 2: All definitions and theorems are therefore insensitive to null sets; we work with equivalence classes. Final answer: Almost everywhere.

prompt: Hölder's inequality states that if 1/p + 1/q = 1 (p,q conjugate exponents), then ∫ |f g| dμ ≤ ||f||_p ||g||_q . When p=q=2 this reduces to the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality for the inner product on L^2. Hölder is the basic tool for estimating products and for identifying duals of L^p spaces.
choices: True only when μ is finite.
False; Hölder is only for p=1.
explanation: Step 1: The proof uses Young's inequality a b ≤ a^p /p + b^q /q on |f| / ||f||_p and |g| / ||g||_q , then integrates. Step 2: The case p=2 is the familiar Cauchy-Schwarz. Final answer: True.

prompt: Minkowski's inequality (triangle inequality for the L^p norm) ||f+g||_p ≤ ||f||_p + ||g||_p is proved using Hölder (for p>1) by writing |f+g|^p = |f+g| · |f+g|^{p-1} and applying Hölder to the two factors with exponents p and q. For p=1 it is immediate from the triangle inequality for the integral. This inequality is what makes ||·||_p a norm.
choices: False; Minkowski holds only for p=2.
False; the L^p 'norm' does not satisfy the triangle inequality.
explanation: Step 1: The algebraic manipulation reduces the p-power of the sum to a product to which Hölder applies, yielding the desired bound after taking p-th roots. Step 2: The case p=1 is just ∫ |f+g| ≤ ∫ |f| + ∫ |g|. Final answer: True.

prompt: L^p spaces are complete (Banach spaces): a Cauchy sequence in L^p norm has a subsequence that converges pointwise a.e. to a limit f ∈ L^p, and the whole sequence converges to f in L^p norm. The proof relies on the fact that the series of differences has ∑ ||f_{n_{k+1}} − f_{n_k}||_p^p < ∞, hence the series converges absolutely a.e., giving pointwise convergence a.e., plus Fatou or monotone convergence to pass to the limit in the norm. Completeness is one of the main reasons L^p spaces are so useful.
choices: False; L^p spaces are never complete.
False; completeness requires the measure to be finite.
explanation: Step 1: Cauchy in norm implies a subsequence with summable differences in norm; the scalar series of p-powers converges, so the series of functions converges a.e. (comparison with integrable majorant or direct). Step 2: Fatou on the tails shows the limit is in L^p and the original sequence converges to it. Final answer: True.

prompt: For 1 < p < ∞ the dual space of L^p is L^q (1/p + 1/q =1): every bounded linear functional on L^p is given by integration against a unique function in L^q. Moreover L^p is reflexive (the canonical embedding into the bidual is surjective). For p=1 the dual is L^∞, but the dual of L^∞ is strictly larger than L^1. Reflexivity for the intermediate p makes many weak-convergence and minimization arguments work smoothly in L^p.
choices: False; the dual of L^p is always L^p.
False; L^p spaces are never reflexive.
explanation: Step 1: The map g ↦ (f ↦ ∫ f g) is an isometric isomorphism from L^q onto (L^p)^* for 1<p<∞ (Hölder's inequality gives the bound; approximation and Radon-Nikodym or other arguments give surjectivity). Step 2: Reflexivity follows because the dual of L^q is L^p. Final answer: True.

prompt: On a finite-measure space (e.g. probability space), if 1 ≤ p < q ≤ ∞ then L^q ⊂ L^p with continuous inclusion ||f||_p ≤ C ||f||_q (C depending on the measure of the space). This follows from Hölder or Jensen's inequality applied to |f|^p = |f|^p · 1. On infinite-measure spaces the inclusions go the other way in the appropriate sense (L^p ∩ L^∞ sits inside L^q for p<q). The dependence on the measure space is crucial when comparing integrability.
choices: False; on finite-measure spaces L^p ⊂ L^q for p<q.
True only when the measure is a probability.
False; there are never inclusions between different L^p spaces.
explanation: Step 1: Write |f|^p = |f|^p · 1 and apply Hölder with exponents q/p and (q/p)' ; the second factor is the measure to a power. Step 2: The constant depends on μ(X). On infinite spaces the constant would be infinite, and indeed the inclusion fails. Final answer: True.

prompt: Plancherel's theorem (the Fourier transform is an isometry on L^2) is the p=2 case of the Hausdorff-Young inequality and is fundamental in harmonic analysis. It says that the L^2 norm of the Fourier transform equals the L^2 norm of the function (up to constants depending on normalization). The proof for Schwartz functions is by direct computation or density plus Parseval for Gaussians; the general L^2 statement follows by density and completeness of L^2. Many other L^p bounds for operators (singular integrals, Fourier restriction, etc.) are proved by interpolation between L^2 (Plancherel) and L^1 or L^∞ endpoints.
choices: False; Plancherel holds only for continuous functions.
False; there is no L^2 theory for the Fourier transform.
explanation: Step 1: On Schwartz space the Fourier transform preserves the L^2 norm by direct calculation (or by showing it is unitary on L^2 via Gaussians and density). Step 2: Density of Schwartz in L^2 plus completeness extends the isometry to all of L^2. Interpolation then yields other L^p bounds. Final answer: True.

prompt: The complex exponentials e^{i n x} (n ∈ ℤ) are orthogonal on [−π,π] (or the circle) with respect to the L^2 inner product. The Fourier coefficient of a function f is the inner product of f against the normalized exponential. Trigonometric polynomials are finite sums of these harmonics and are dense in L^2 of the circle.
choices: False; the exponentials are not orthogonal.
False; trigonometric polynomials are never dense in L^2.
explanation: Step 1: Direct computation: ∫ e^{i(n-m)x} dx over a period is 2π δ_{n m}. Step 2: Completeness of the system in L^2 follows from density of continuous functions (Stone-Weierstrass) and Fejér or other approximation. Final answer: True.

prompt: For an L^2 function the partial sums of its Fourier series are the orthogonal projections onto the span of the first few harmonics. Consequently the L^2 norm of the partial sum is at most the L^2 norm of f (Bessel), and the error is orthogonal to the span. This is the starting point for all L^2 theory of Fourier series.
choices: False; partial sums are not projections.
True only when f is a trigonometric polynomial.
False; there is no orthogonality in L^2.
explanation: Step 1: The coefficient formulas are exactly the inner-product formulas for orthogonal projection onto the finite-dimensional span. Step 2: Pythagoras then gives ||S_N||_2^2 + ||f − S_N||_2^2 = ||f||_2^2 , which implies Bessel's inequality and, with completeness, Parseval. Final answer: True.

prompt: If f is periodic, integrable, and of bounded variation, then at every point the Fourier partial sums converge to the average of the left and right limits of f. In particular, at every point of continuity the series converges to f(x). This is Jordan's theorem (a strengthening of Dirichlet's theorem). The proof uses the Dirichlet kernel and integration by parts on the resulting integral (or summation by parts).
choices: False; BV is not needed for pointwise convergence.
False; Fourier series never converge pointwise for BV functions.
explanation: Step 1: The Dirichlet kernel integral against f can be integrated by parts when f has bounded variation (the integrated term is the variation, the remainder is a Riemann-Lebesgue term that goes to 0). Step 2: The integrated term produces the average of the jumps. Final answer: True.

prompt: Even for continuous functions the Fourier series need not converge pointwise everywhere (du Bois-Reymond constructed a continuous function whose series diverges at a point). However, the Fejér (Cesàro) means always converge uniformly to the function when f is continuous. This shows that while the raw series may misbehave, a simple averaging procedure recovers the function uniformly from the coefficients.
choices: False; continuous functions always have uniformly convergent Fourier series.
False; Fejér means never converge for continuous functions.
explanation: Step 1: The du Bois-Reymond example is a classical (difficult) construction using a lacunary series or condensation of singularities. Step 2: Fejér kernel is a positive approximate identity; convolution with it produces trigonometric polynomials that converge uniformly to any continuous periodic f by the usual argument for approximate identities. Final answer: True.

prompt: For any f in L^2 of the circle the Fourier partial sums converge to f in L^2 norm, and Parseval's identity holds: the L^2 norm squared of f equals the sum of |ĉ(n)|^2 . This follows from the fact that trigonometric polynomials are dense in L^2 and the partial sums are the orthogonal projections. Parseval is the Plancherel theorem for the circle.
choices: False; L^2 convergence requires f continuous.
False; Parseval holds only for finite Fourier series.
explanation: Step 1: Density + orthogonality of the system implies that the projections S_N f converge in L^2 to f. Step 2: Taking the limit in ||S_N f||^2 = sum_{|n|≤N} |ĉ(n)|^2 gives Parseval. Final answer: True.

prompt: Parseval's identity for the square wave or for f(x)=x on (−π,π) yields famous numerical series (e.g. sum 1/k^2 = π²/6). In general, Parseval converts L^2 information about a function into information about the decay or size of its Fourier coefficients, and vice versa. This is one of the most useful computational and theoretical tools in Fourier analysis.
choices: False; Parseval never gives exact values for series.
True only when the function is a polynomial.
False; one cannot recover series from Parseval.
explanation: Step 1: For f(x)=x the coefficients are explicit (∼1/k for the sine terms); Parseval equates the L^2 norm (easy integral) to the sum of squares of coefficients, producing ζ(2)=π²/6 after algebra. Step 2: The same works for many other explicit functions. Final answer: True.

prompt: The Fejér means (Cesàro averages of the partial sums) of the Fourier series of a continuous periodic function converge uniformly to the function. The Fejér kernel is nonnegative, has integral 1, and concentrates at 0; convolution with it is therefore an approximate identity that reproduces continuous functions uniformly. This gives a constructive proof that trigonometric polynomials are dense in the uniform norm on the circle.
choices: False; Fejér means converge only pointwise, not uniformly.
False; trigonometric polynomials are not dense uniformly.
explanation: Step 1: Nonnegative kernel + integral 1 + concentration ⇒ convolution approximates the identity uniformly on continuous periodic functions (standard ε/3 argument). Step 2: The convolution is exactly the Fejér mean, a trigonometric polynomial. Final answer: True.

prompt: Near a jump discontinuity the Fourier partial sums exhibit the Gibbs phenomenon: they overshoot the target value by a fixed percentage (roughly 9% of the jump height on each side) no matter how large the degree N becomes. The width of the overshoot region shrinks like 1/N, but the relative height stays constant. This is purely a local phenomenon caused by the slow decay of the Dirichlet kernel sidelobes.
choices: False; the overshoot disappears as N→∞.
True only for discontinuous functions of bounded variation.
False; Gibbs phenomenon affects only L^2 convergence.
explanation: Step 1: The integral of the Dirichlet kernel against the jump produces a fixed oscillatory integral (the sine integral Si(π) or similar) whose value is greater than the target by the Gibbs constant ≈0.089. Step 2: As N→∞ the oscillation squeezes to the discontinuity but the max/min of the partial sum stay at the same relative height. Final answer: True.

prompt: Carleson's theorem states that for any square-integrable function on the circle the Fourier series converges pointwise almost everywhere to the function. This is a deep result (1966); the L^1 case is still open in the sense that there exist L^1 functions whose Fourier series diverge on a set of positive measure, but a.e. convergence for L^1 remains a major open problem. The proof of Carleson uses sophisticated time-frequency analysis and Carleson measures.
choices: False; Fourier series of L^2 functions always converge everywhere.
False; Carleson's theorem is for the Fourier transform on ℝ.
explanation: Step 1: Carleson proved a.e. pointwise convergence for L^2; Hunt extended to L^p , p>1. Step 2: Kolmogorov's example shows divergence on a positive-measure set is possible for some L^1 functions; whether every L^1 function has a.e. convergence is still open. Final answer: True.

prompt: The solution of the heat equation on the circle with initial data f is obtained by multiplying each Fourier coefficient ĉ_n by the decaying factor e^{-n^2 t}. High frequencies are damped instantly; the solution becomes instantly smooth for t>0. This is the prototypical example of how Fourier series 'diagonalize' constant-coefficient linear PDEs with periodic boundary conditions.
choices: False; the heat equation cannot be solved with Fourier series.
False; the solution remains as rough as the initial data.
explanation: Step 1: Expand the initial data; each eigenfunction e^{i n x} of the Laplacian on the circle has eigenvalue −n^2 and therefore evolves by the factor e^{-n^2 t} under the heat equation. Step 2: The series with the damped coefficients solves the PDE and satisfies the initial condition (in L^2 or better). Final answer: True.

prompt: A map f : U ⊂ ℝ^n → ℝ^m is differentiable at a if there is a linear map L such that f(a+h) = f(a) + L h + o(||h||) as h→0. When the partial derivatives exist and are continuous in a neighborhood, f is differentiable and L is given by the Jacobian matrix. Existence of partials alone does not imply differentiability.
choices: False; existence of partials always implies differentiability.
False; continuity of partials is necessary for differentiability.
explanation: Step 1: The definition is the little-o approximation by a linear map (the candidate derivative). Step 2: The mean-value theorem applied coordinatewise shows that continuous partials give the little-o remainder. Counter-examples exist when partials exist but are discontinuous. Final answer: True.

prompt: The function f(x,y) = (x y)/(x² + y²) for (x,y)≠(0,0), f(0,0)=0, has all directional derivatives at 0 equal to 0, yet is not differentiable at 0. Along the line y = m x the difference quotient tends to m/(1+m²), which depends on the direction m. Therefore no single linear map L can serve as the derivative. This shows that directional derivatives existing in all directions is not sufficient for differentiability in several variables.
choices: False; the function is differentiable at 0 with derivative the zero map.
True only in dimensions greater than 2.
False; all partial derivatives exist and are continuous.
explanation: Step 1: The directional derivative in direction v is lim t→0 [f(t v) − f(0)] / t ; for this f it is 0 along every fixed direction. Step 2: The remainder term divided by ||h|| does not tend to 0 uniformly in direction; the limit depends on the slope, so no linear L works. Final answer: True.

prompt: The chain rule in several variables states that D(f ∘ g)(a) = Df(g(a)) ∘ Dg(a) (composition of the linear maps, i.e., product of Jacobian matrices). It is proved by writing the little-o definitions for f and g and composing the approximations. This is the multivariable version of the one-variable chain rule and is the basis for all differentiation of composite maps.
choices: False; there is no chain rule in several variables.
True only when the maps are linear.
False; the chain rule requires the maps to be C^1.
explanation: Step 1: f(g(a+h)) = f(g(a)) + Df(g(a)) (g(a+h)−g(a)) + o(||g(a+h)−g(a)||). Step 2: Substitute the approximation g(a+h)−g(a) = Dg(a) h + o(||h||); the little-o terms compose correctly because ||g(a+h)−g(a)|| = O(||h||). Final answer: True.

prompt: The inverse function theorem says that if f is C^1 near a and Df(a) is invertible, then f is locally a C^1 diffeomorphism near a (invertible with C^1 inverse). The proof reduces the problem to finding a fixed point of a contraction (or uses the Newton method in Banach spaces) after a preliminary linear change of variables. The same idea, with a little more bookkeeping, yields the implicit function theorem. These theorems are the rigorous foundation for 'solving' equations locally when the linearization is nondegenerate.
choices: False; the inverse function theorem requires f to be linear.
False; invertibility of Df is not needed.
explanation: Step 1: After translating so a=0 and f(0)=0, one considers the map g(y) = y − A^{-1} f(y) where A=Df(0) is invertible; a fixed point of g is a solution of f(y)=0. Step 2: On a small ball the contraction constant can be made <1 by continuity of Df. The implicit function theorem is proved by applying the inverse function theorem to a suitable auxiliary map. Final answer: True.

prompt: Fubini's theorem: if f is integrable over a product measure space (∫∫ |f| dμ dν < ∞), then the iterated integrals equal the double integral, and for almost every fixed value of one variable the slice is integrable. Tonelli's theorem removes the absolute integrability requirement when f ≥ 0 (the iterated integrals, possibly +∞, still equal the double integral). These theorems justify changing the order of integration and reducing multiple integrals to iterated one-dimensional integrals.
choices: False; Fubini requires the functions to be continuous.
True only when both measures are finite.
False; changing order of integration is never justified.
explanation: Step 1: For nonnegative f, Tonelli follows from the monotone convergence theorem applied to simple functions approximating f. Step 2: For signed integrable f one splits into positive and negative parts and applies Tonelli; the absolute integrability guarantees that the iterated integrals of the parts are finite. Final answer: True.

prompt: The function f(x,y) = (x−y)/((x+y)^3) on (0,1)×(0,1) (suitably defined on the diagonal) has the property that the two iterated integrals exist and are finite but are unequal. This shows that without absolute integrability (Fubini hypothesis) the order of integration can matter. The example is a standard warning that 'formal' manipulation of double integrals without checking integrability of |f| can lead to paradoxes.
choices: False; iterated integrals of any integrable function always agree.
True only when the domain is unbounded.
False; the function is not measurable.
explanation: Step 1: Direct computation of the inner integrals produces expressions whose further integration yields different values (classically  π/4  and −π/4 or similar after suitable regularization). Step 2: |f| is not integrable over the square; the positive and negative parts each integrate to infinity, so Fubini does not apply. Final answer: True.

prompt: The change-of-variables formula for multiple integrals states that under a C^1 diffeomorphism φ, ∫_V f(y) dy = ∫_U f(φ(x)) |det Dφ(x)| dx . The factor |det Dφ(x)| is the local volume distortion. The absolute value is required because the integral does not care about orientation reversal. The proof reduces via Fubini and a preliminary linear change of variables to the one-variable substitution rule in each coordinate after using the inverse function theorem locally.
choices: False; the Jacobian determinant appears without absolute value.
False; there is no change-of-variables formula in several variables.
explanation: Step 1: For linear maps the formula is immediate from the definition of the determinant as the volume scaling factor (with sign for orientation). Step 2: The general case is localized by the inverse function theorem, reduced to the linear case plus a one-variable substitution via Fubini after a preliminary change that straightens the map. Final answer: True.

prompt: In polar coordinates on ℝ^2 the Jacobian determinant is r, so the area element dx dy becomes r dr dθ. This is the reason the Gaussian integral ∫ e^{-(x²+y²)} dx dy = π is most easily computed in polar coordinates: it becomes ∫_0^∞ e^{-r²} 2π r dr = π after the substitution u=r². The same Jacobian factor appears in every change to 'radial' coordinates in higher dimensions.
choices: False; the Jacobian in polar coordinates is 1.
False; polar coordinates have Jacobian r².
explanation: Step 1: x=r cos θ, y=r sin θ; the matrix of partials has determinant r (the parallelogram spanned by the partials has area r). Step 2: The absolute value gives the positive factor r that converts the Cartesian area element into the polar one. The Gaussian computation is the classic illustration. Final answer: True.

prompt: Stokes' theorem in its general form states that ∫_M dω = ∫_{∂M} ω for a differential form ω on a manifold with boundary M. The classical theorems of vector calculus (fundamental theorem of line integrals, Green's theorem, Stokes' theorem for surfaces, divergence theorem) are all special cases for appropriate choices of the form and the dimension. The theorem is the higher-dimensional, coordinate-free version of the fundamental theorem of calculus.
choices: False; Stokes' theorem holds only in ℝ^3.
False; there is no general Stokes' theorem.
explanation: Step 1: d on 0-forms is the ordinary differential (gradient); Stokes reduces to the fundamental theorem. Step 2: In ℝ^3 the exterior derivative encodes grad, curl, div, and the theorem reproduces the classical integral theorems. The general proof uses partitions of unity and reduction to the one-variable FTC in charts. Final answer: True.

prompt: The exterior derivative satisfies d² = 0. In ℝ^3 this is equivalent to curl(grad f) = 0 and div(curl F) = 0. This identity is the reason that 'exact forms are closed' and underlies the de Rham cohomology theory that measures the topological obstructions to solving dω = α. It is also the source of the vector-calculus identities that make the classical Stokes theorems consistent (the boundary of a boundary is empty, so applying d twice gives zero on both sides).
explanation: Step 1: The formula for d on a k-form involves an alternating sum; applying it twice produces pairwise cancellations with opposite signs. Step 2: In coordinates this is immediate; globally it is the same algebraic identity. The topological consequence is that if α = dω then dα = 0, so closed but not exact forms detect topology. Final answer: True.
